before_exam_checklist: Revise all analogy relationship types — practise forming bridge sentences
Learn 5–10 new vocabulary words every week from the master list
Read a quality book, newspaper, or magazine article daily
Do timed verbal reasoning practice papers under exam conditions
Review all prefix, suffix, and root word lists until automatic
Practise spotting context clues in reading passages

time_management: Answer questions you're confident about first
Spend no more than 45 seconds per question before moving on
Mark questions you're unsure about and return if time allows
Never leave a multiple choice question blank — make your best guess
reading_strategy: For passages: skim-read first, then answer questions with targeted re-reading
Underline or mentally note key words in both question and passage
For analogy questions: form your bridge sentence BEFORE looking at options
elimination_strategy: Eliminate options you're certain are wrong
If two options remain, compare them directly — look for the subtle difference
Trust your process over your instinct if the two conflict

common_traps.trap: Choosing a synonym instead of an antonym
common_traps.fix: Re-read the question: does it ask for same meaning or opposite meaning?

common_traps.trap: Picking an 'associated' word in analogies instead of the 'related' word
common_traps.example: AUTHOR : NOVEL :: COMPOSER : Piano (associated) instead of Symphony (product)
common_traps.fix: Always apply the EXACT relationship from A:B to find D

common_traps.example: Concluding the character is 'devastated' when the text only says she 'frowned'
common_traps.fix: Stay close to what the text actually says — only infer what is strongly implied

common_traps.example: Thinking 'let the cat out of the bag' involves an actual cat
common_traps.fix: Ask: does the literal meaning make sense in context? If not, it's an idiom

common_traps.trap: Treating 'all' and 'some' as interchangeable in logic
common_traps.example: Some athletes are fast → All athletes are fast (WRONG)
common_traps.fix: Be precise with quantifiers: all, some, none, most, a few all mean different things

common_traps.example: All dogs are animals → All animals are dogs (WRONG)
common_traps.fix: The relationship only works one way unless stated otherwise

common_traps.trap: Using the most common word meaning when context implies another
common_traps.example: 'Buoy' chosen as a float (noun) when used as a verb meaning 'to lift'
common_traps.fix: Always determine part of speech and use context to find the intended meaning

comprehension_question_type_strategies.literal: Find it directly in the text
comprehension_question_type_strategies.inferential: Reason from clues in the text
comprehension_question_type_strategies.main_idea: Look at first and last paragraphs
comprehension_question_type_strategies.author_tone: Look for emotive and evaluative language

practice.explanation: Pain is caused by injury. Grief is caused by loss.

practice.question: Some children eat pears. Nobody who eats pears likes oranges. All who like oranges like broccoli. Which must be true?
options: Some children do not like oranges
All children like pears and oranges
practice.answer: Some children do not like oranges
solutionSteps: Some children eat pears → those children don't like oranges
Therefore some children do not like oranges ✓

exam.name: Victorian Selective Entry Examination
exam.subject: Quantitative Reasoning
exam.description: Comprehensive study notes to master all quantitative reasoning problem types for the Victorian Selective Entry Exam.
exam.usage_guide: Read each section carefully. Attempt example problems before reading the solution. Revisit tips regularly. Practise timed papers to build speed.
exam.golden_rule: Read every question twice. Selective exam questions often contain a subtle condition (e.g., 'which is NOT true', 'how many MORE than') that completely changes the answer.

topics.title: Arithmetic with Integers
identification_cues: The question involves whole numbers (positive or negative)
You see operations: +, −, ×, ÷
Key words: total, difference, product, quotient, sum, how many more
Often disguised as a word problem: 'a bank account starts at −$45 and gains $120…'

examples.question: A submarine is at −240 m. It rises 75 m, then descends 130 m. What is its final depth?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Number lines help with negatives — draw one mentally
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Visualise negative numbers and inequalities by placing values on a mental number line.
tips_and_tricks.example: -3 + 5: start at -3, move 5 right → land on 2. Or: -3 is left of -1, so -3 < -1

tips_and_tricks.tip: For large multiplications, break apart: 48 × 25 = 48 × 100 ÷ 4 = 4800 ÷ 4 = 1200
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Always check: does your answer make sense in context?

worked_example.question: A submarine is at −240 m. It rises 75 m, then descends 130 m. What is its final depth?
steps: Always check: does your answer make sense in context? → Start: −240
Answer: −295 m

topics.concept_explanation: Direct computation with negative numbers, large numbers, or tricky sequences of operations.
common_mistakes: Making sign errors with negative numbers, especially (−) × (−) = (+)
Not following BODMAS order — brackets and powers before multiplication/division
Rushing and making careless arithmetic errors under time pressure
Not estimating first to check whether the answer is reasonable

topics.title: Fractions, Decimals & Percentages
identification_cues: You see fractions (a/b), decimals (0.xx), or the % symbol
Key words: of, out of, percent, proportion, fraction of
'Which is largest: 3/7, 0.44, or 42%?'

examples.question: A jacket costs $180. It is reduced by 15%, then the sale price is increased by 10%. What is the final price?
examples.key_insight: A 15% decrease followed by 10% increase is NOT the same as a 5% decrease!

tips_and_tricks.explanation: Knowing common fraction-decimal equivalents lets you compare and convert instantly.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Quick % calculations: 10% = move decimal left one place; 5% = half of 10%; 1% = move decimal left two places
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Build up percentages from easy ones: 10% (÷10), 5% (half of 10%), 1% (÷100).

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Of' always means multiply: '30% of 50' = 0.30 × 50 = 15
tips_and_tricks.explanation: In percentage/fraction problems, the word "of" always means multiplication.

steps: Identify which representation is easiest for the problem
Convert between forms as needed: fraction ↔ decimal ↔ percentage
For comparison: convert all to the same form (usually decimals)

worked_example.question: A jacket costs $180. It is reduced by 15%, then the sale price is increased by 10%. What is the final price?
steps: Identify which representation is easiest for the problem → After 15% reduction: 180 × 0.85 = $153
Convert between forms as needed: fraction ↔ decimal ↔ percentage → After 10% increase: 153 × 1.10 = $168.30
For comparison: convert all to the same form (usually decimals)

topics.concept_explanation: Converting between forms, ordering fractions, finding a percentage of a quantity, percentage increase/decrease.
key_formulas: Percentage of a quantity: (P/100) × amount
Percentage change: (change ÷ original) × 100
New value after X% increase: original × (1 + X/100)
New value after X% decrease: original × (1 − X/100)
common_mistakes: Not knowing key fraction-decimal-percentage equivalences by heart (1/8 = 0.125 = 12.5%)
Adding fractions without finding a common denominator
Calculating percentage of a number incorrectly (e.g., 15% of 80 ≠ 80 ÷ 15)
Confusing 'what percentage is A of B' (A/B × 100) with 'what is A% of B' (A/100 × B)

topics.title: Ratios & Proportions
identification_cues: You see a colon ratio (e.g., 3:4:5) or the phrase 'for every'
Problems involve sharing, mixing, or scaling
solving_steps_sharing: Add all parts of the ratio together
Divide the total by the number of parts to get 1 part

proportion_types.description: More of one → more of the other
More of one → less of the other

examples.question: Blue, red, and yellow paint are mixed in the ratio 3:5:2. If 240 mL of red paint is used, what is the total volume?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always label your parts. Write '1 part = ___' explicitly.
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Equivalent ratios: 2:3 = 4:6 = 6:9. Simplify by dividing by HCF
tips_and_tricks.explanation: HCF uses the LOWEST powers because we want what both numbers share.
tips_and_tricks.example: 12 = 2² × 3, 18 = 2 × 3². Common primes: 2¹ × 3¹ = 6

steps: If asked for ratio of X to Y, write X:Y, not Y:X

worked_example.question: Blue, red, and yellow paint are mixed in the ratio 3:5:2. If 240 mL of red paint is used, what is the total volume?
steps: If asked for ratio of X to Y, write X:Y, not Y:X → Red = 5 parts = 240 mL → 1 part = 48 mL
Answer: 480 mL

topics.concept_explanation: Sharing an amount in a given ratio, scaling up recipes, direct and inverse proportion.
common_mistakes: Confusing the order of the ratio — 2:3 boys to girls is different from 3:2
Not simplifying ratios or cross-multiplying correctly
Forgetting to add ratio parts to find the total shares
Applying ratios to the wrong quantity (e.g., ratio of boys to total vs boys to girls)

topics.title: Powers, Roots & Order of Operations
identification_cues: You see exponent notation (², ³, ⁴) or square root symbols (√)
The question has multiple operations mixed together

examples.question: Calculate: 4 + 3² × (8 − 5) ÷ 9

tips_and_tricks.tip: Common trap: −3² ≠ (−3)². Without brackets: −(3²) = −9. With brackets: (−3)² = +9
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Memorise squares up to 15² = 225
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Knowing perfect squares up to 225 helps with factoring, surds, and mental estimation.
tips_and_tricks.example: 12 squared = 144, 13 squared = 169, 14 squared = 196, 15 squared = 225

tips_and_tricks.tip: Memorise cubes 1³ through 5³
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Perfect cubes come up in volume problems and simplifying cube roots.
tips_and_tricks.example: 2 cubed = 8, 3 cubed = 27, 4 cubed = 64, 5 cubed = 125

steps: Write the ratio in the order stated in the question
Simplify by dividing by the HCF
For sharing: find total parts → value of one part → value of each share
For proportion: set up equivalent ratios and cross-multiply

worked_example.question: Calculate: 4 + 3² × (8 − 5) ÷ 9
steps: Write the ratio in the order stated in the question → Brackets: 8 − 5 = 3 → 4 + 3² × 3 ÷ 9
Simplify by dividing by the HCF → Orders: 3² = 9 → 4 + 9 × 3 ÷ 9
For sharing: find total parts → value of one part → value of each share → Multiply: 9 × 3 = 27 → 4 + 27 ÷ 9
For proportion: set up equivalent ratios and cross-multiply → Divide: 27 ÷ 9 = 3 → 4 + 3

topics.concept_explanation: Evaluating expressions with exponents, roots, and mixed operations using BODMAS.
common_mistakes: Evaluating left to right without respecting BODMAS order
Confusing 2³ (= 8) with 3² (= 9) — base and exponent are not interchangeable
Not computing brackets first in expressions like 3 × (4 + 2)²
Forgetting that −3² = −9 but (−3)² = 9

topics.title: Number Sequences
identification_cues: A row or column of numbers with a blank or question mark
Asked for: next term, missing number, or rule of the sequence

sequence_types.rule: each term = sum of previous two

examples.question: Find the next two terms: 3, 4, 6, 9, 13, ___, ___
What comes next: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
Find the next term: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ?
What comes next: 3, 5, 9, 15, 23, ?
Find the missing number: 2, 5, 11, 23, ?, 95

tips_and_tricks.tip: If stuck after 1st differences, find 2nd differences. If constant, rule involves n²
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check if adjacent terms have a constant ratio (multiply/divide)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

steps: Always write out differences between terms first — most powerful technique
For alternating sequences, look at every other term separately

worked_example.question: What comes next: 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
steps: Always write out differences between terms first — most powerful technique → Each term × 3: 2→6→18→54
For alternating sequences, look at every other term separately → Next: 54 × 3 = 162

topics.concept_explanation: A series of numbers where you must find the next term(s) or a missing term.
common_mistakes: Assuming the pattern is always simple addition — check multiplication, squares, and other operations
Not checking second differences when first differences are not constant
Looking at too few terms to identify the pattern reliably
Not considering interleaved sequences (odd positions follow one rule, even positions another)

topics.title: Shape / Figural Sequences
identification_cues: Diagrams with shapes, dots, or tiles
Asked for: total count at step N, or the rule

examples.question: Figure 1 has 1 tile, Figure 2 has 5, Figure 3 has 13, Figure 4 has 25. How many tiles in Figure 6?
A pattern adds 3 dots per step: Step 1 has 1 dot, Step 2 has 4 dots. How many dots at Step 10?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always build a table of values
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: If 2nd differences are constant, the formula involves n²
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Draw or imagine Figure 0 — sometimes the formula becomes obvious
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: sometimes the formula becomes obvious

steps: Write out the first few terms and look for a pattern
Calculate differences between consecutive terms
If differences are constant → arithmetic sequence
If differences grow → try second differences or multiplication patterns

worked_example.question: Figure 1 has 1 tile, Figure 2 has 5, Figure 3 has 13, Figure 4 has 25. How many tiles in Figure 6?
steps: Write out the first few terms and look for a pattern → Differences: 4, 8, 12 → 2nd differences: 4, 4 (constant) → quadratic
Calculate differences between consecutive terms → Coefficient of n²: 4/2 = 2
If differences are constant → arithmetic sequence → Formula: 2n² − 2n + 1. Check: n=1 → 1 ✓, n=2 → 5 ✓
If differences grow → try second differences or multiplication patterns → Figure 6: 2(36) − 2(6) + 1 = 72 − 12 + 1 = 61
Answer: 61 tiles

topics.concept_explanation: A series of figures that change progressively — find a count at step N or the rule.
common_mistakes: Focusing on only one aspect of the change while missing other simultaneous patterns
Confusing clockwise and anticlockwise rotation directions
Not checking your predicted answer against ALL previous figures in the sequence
Overlooking small details like dots, lines, or shading changes

topics.title: Shape Rotations
identification_cues: Which figure shows the shape after a 90° clockwise turn?
What does this pattern look like rotated?
Select the rotated version
key_rules: 90° clockwise: top goes to right, right goes to bottom, bottom goes to left, left goes to top
180°: everything flips upside-down AND left-right
270° clockwise = 90° anticlockwise
360° = back to original

examples.question: An L-shape points up-right. After 90° clockwise rotation, which direction does it point?
solution_steps: Up-right becomes right-down, which is down-right

examples.question: A triangle with a dot on its top-left corner is rotated 180°. Where is the dot now?
A flag shape 🏳 pointing right is rotated 90° anticlockwise, then reflected in a vertical line. What is the final orientation?
solution_steps: Reflect vertically: flag still points up (vertical reflection flips left/right, not up/down)
Wait — a flag pointing up, reflected in vertical axis: the flag part flips side. But the direction (up) stays.
Actually: 90° ACW makes it point up. Vertical reflection keeps it pointing up but mirrors the flag detail.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Use your hand or a small piece of paper to physically rotate — it's faster than imagining
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This shortcut saves valuable time in timed exams while maintaining accuracy.
tips_and_tricks.example: it's faster than imagining

tips_and_tricks.tip: For 180°, just flip everything: top↔bottom AND left↔right
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 90° CW tip: turn the page 90° clockwise and see what the shape looks like
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Always check at least 2 features (e.g., a dot AND a corner) to avoid mistakes

worked_example.question: An L-shape points up-right. After 90° clockwise rotation, which direction does it point?
steps: Always check at least 2 features (e.g., a dot AND a corner) to avoid mistakes → Top → right, right → bottom
Answer: Down-right

topics.concept_explanation: Identifying what a shape looks like after being rotated 90°, 180°, or 270° around a point.
common_mistakes: Confusing rotation with reflection — rotation turns, reflection flips
Rotating in the wrong direction (clockwise vs anticlockwise)
Not tracking individual points or features through the rotation
Forgetting that 90° clockwise is the same as 270° anticlockwise

topics.title: Reflections & Symmetry
identification_cues: Which is the reflection of this shape?
How many lines of symmetry does this shape have?
Which figure is the mirror image?
key_rules: Vertical mirror: left↔right swap, top/bottom stay
Horizontal mirror: top↔bottom swap, left/right stay
Reflection reverses handedness — a clockwise spiral becomes anticlockwise
Lines of symmetry: circle=∞, square=4, rectangle=2, equilateral triangle=3, regular pentagon=5

examples.question: The letter 'F' is reflected in a vertical mirror line. What does it look like?
solution_steps: Vertical reflection swaps left and right
The horizontal lines of F now extend to the left instead of right
It looks like a backwards F

examples.question: How many lines of symmetry does a regular hexagon have?
solution_steps: Regular hexagon: 6 equal sides, 6 equal angles
Lines through midpoints of opposite sides: 3

examples.question: A shape is reflected in a horizontal line, then reflected in a vertical line. The result is the same as which single transformation?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Reflections REVERSE left/right or top/bottom but NOT both (that's rotation)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Text and asymmetric features are the giveaway — look for reversed letters/numbers
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: look for reversed letters/numbers

tips_and_tricks.tip: Two reflections in perpendicular lines = 180° rotation
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Use a ruler or pencil as the mirror line and imagine folding the paper

worked_example.question: The letter 'F' is reflected in a vertical mirror line. What does it look like?
steps: Use a ruler or pencil as the mirror line and imagine folding the paper → Vertical reflection swaps left and right
Answer: Backwards F (⌐)

topics.concept_explanation: Identifying mirror images of shapes across horizontal, vertical, or diagonal lines.
common_mistakes: Confusing reflection with rotation — reflections flip, rotations turn
Not maintaining equal perpendicular distance from the mirror line for all points
Forgetting that reflected text reads backwards
Confusing vertical and horizontal mirror lines

topics.title: Paper Folding & Hole Punching
identification_cues: A paper is folded and a hole is punched. What does it look like unfolded?
Which figure shows the result of folding along the dotted line?
Paper is folded twice then cut. How many holes?

examples.question: A square paper is folded in half (left to right). A hole is punched in the centre of the folded paper. How many holes when unfolded?
solution_steps: Holes are symmetric about the fold line (left-right mirror)

examples.question: A square paper is folded in half (top to bottom), then folded in half again (left to right). A hole is punched in the top-right corner. How many holes when fully unfolded?
solution_steps: Unfold left-right: hole mirrors to top-left
Unfold top-bottom: both holes mirror to bottom
4 holes, one in each corner area

examples.question: A square paper is folded diagonally (bottom-left to top-right), then a semicircle is cut from the folded edge. What shape appears when unfolded?
solution_steps: Cut on the fold edge goes through both layers
When unfolded, the two semicircles join to form a full circle

examples.question: Paper is folded in half 3 times, then a single hole is punched. How many holes appear when fully unfolded?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Rule: n folds = 2ⁿ layers = 2ⁿ holes per punch
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always track the fold direction — it determines where the mirror copies appear
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: it determines where the mirror copies appear

tips_and_tricks.tip: For cuts on the fold edge: the cut mirrors perfectly along the fold
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Practice with actual paper — fold, punch, unfold to build intuition
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: fold, punch, unfold to build intuition

steps: Trace the fold line and imagine flipping one half onto the other
Holes punched go through ALL layers — count the layers at each point
Unfold step by step in reverse order
Symmetry: each fold creates a mirror line

worked_example.question: A square paper is folded in half (left to right). A hole is punched in the centre of the folded paper. How many holes when unfolded?
steps: Trace the fold line and imagine flipping one half onto the other → 1 fold = 2 layers
Holes punched go through ALL layers — count the layers at each point → 1 punch through 2 layers = 2 holes
Unfold step by step in reverse order → Holes are symmetric about the fold line (left-right mirror)
Symmetry: each fold creates a mirror line

topics.concept_explanation: Predicting what a folded paper looks like when unfolded, or where holes appear after punching through folded paper.
common_mistakes: Not reflecting the hole/cut across EACH fold line in the correct order (unfold in reverse)
Losing track of which corner is which after multiple folds
Forgetting that the number of holes doubles with each fold (one fold = 2 holes, two folds = 4)
Incorrectly reflecting — the hole position mirrors across the fold line, not across the page

topics.title: Visual Pattern Completion
identification_cues: Which completes the pattern?
What comes next in this visual sequence?
Which replaces the question mark?

examples.question: In a 3×3 grid, each row shows a circle with 1, 2, 3 dots. Each column shows the circle in white, grey, black. The bottom-right cell is missing. What is it?
solution_steps: Row 3 pattern: 1 dot, 2 dots, ? dots → 3 dots

examples.question: A sequence shows: △, □, ○, △, □, ○, △, □, ? What comes next?
In a 3×3 grid, each row has shapes that rotate 45° clockwise, and shading alternates. Row 3 has: dark arrow pointing NE, light arrow pointing E, ?. What fills the cell?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Common rules: rotation, count change, shading cycle, size change, element addition/removal
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Some patterns combine two rules (e.g., rotate + change shading)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: rotate + change shading)

steps: ALWAYS check rows AND columns — the pattern must work in both directions
If you can't see the pattern in rows, try columns or diagonals

worked_example.question: In a 3×3 grid, each row shows a circle with 1, 2, 3 dots. Each column shows the circle in white, grey, black. The bottom-right cell is missing. What is it?
steps: ALWAYS check rows AND columns — the pattern must work in both directions → Row 3 pattern: 1 dot, 2 dots, ? dots → 3 dots
If you can't see the pattern in rows, try columns or diagonals → Column 3 pattern: white, grey, ? → black
Answer: Black circle, 3 dots

topics.concept_explanation: Finding the missing piece in a visual pattern grid (typically 3×3 with one cell missing).
common_mistakes: Only checking rows OR columns — you must verify the pattern works in both directions
Overlooking subtle differences between similar-looking options
Assuming the same type of pattern applies to all elements within each figure
Not checking your answer against all rows and columns before selecting

topics.title: 3D Nets & Visualization
identification_cues: Which net folds into this cube?
What 3D shape does this net make?
Which face is opposite the shaded face?

examples.question: A cross-shaped net of 6 squares folds into a cube. The centre square is the base. Which square becomes the top?
solution_steps: In a cross net: centre = base
The square directly opposite (2 squares up from centre, past the fold) becomes the top
The one directly above centre becomes a side face

examples.question: A cube has faces numbered 1-6. In the net: 1 is in the centre. 2 is above, 3 right, 4 below, 5 left, 6 is above 2. What number is opposite 1?
Which of these CANNOT be a net of a cube? A) Cross shape (6 squares) B) T-shape (6 squares) C) L-shape with 4 in a row + 2 on one side D) 2×3 rectangle of squares
solution_steps: D (2×3 rectangle) ✗ — when folded, two faces overlap

tips_and_tricks.tip: There are exactly 11 different nets for a cube — worth recognising the common ones
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: worth recognising the common ones

tips_and_tricks.tip: Quick check: any net with 4+ squares in a straight line is suspicious (only works if extras are spaced right)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Opposite face rule: in a cross net, the face 2 steps away (skipping one) is opposite
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: For non-cube nets: count faces and match to the 3D shape first
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.

steps: Mentally rotate or trace the shape step by step
Use elimination — rule out impossible options first
For rotations: track one distinctive feature (corner, dot, mark)
For reflections: every point flips equal distance across the mirror line

worked_example.question: A cross-shaped net of 6 squares folds into a cube. The centre square is the base. Which square becomes the top?
steps: Mentally rotate or trace the shape step by step → In a cross net: centre = base
Use elimination — rule out impossible options first → The square directly opposite (2 squares up from centre, past the fold) becomes the top
For rotations: track one distinctive feature (corner, dot, mark) → The one directly above centre becomes a side face
For reflections: every point flips equal distance across the mirror line
Answer: The one 2 squares above centre

topics.concept_explanation: Identifying which 3D shape a flat net folds into, or which net folds into a given 3D shape.
common_mistakes: Not correctly identifying opposite faces on a cube net — opposite faces never share an edge
Forgetting that patterns on faces rotate when the net is folded
Not mentally folding systematically — track one face at a time
Confusing valid cube nets with invalid ones (there are exactly 11 valid cube nets)

topics.title: Solving for Unknowns
identification_cues: A letter (x, n, p, □) represents an unknown
An equals sign is present
Asked for: 'find x', 'what is the value of…', 'solve for…'

examples.question: If 5(2x − 3) = 45, what is x?

tips_and_tricks.tip: On multiple choice: sometimes faster to try each answer option (back-solving)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This shortcut saves valuable time in timed exams while maintaining accuracy.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Whatever you do to one side, do to both sides
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Always check your answer by substituting back in

worked_example.question: If 5(2x − 3) = 45, what is x?
steps: Always check your answer by substituting back in → Expand: 10x − 15 = 45
Answer: x = 6

golden_rules: Do the same to both sides — always.
topics.concept_explanation: A mathematical sentence with a missing value represented by a letter.
common_mistakes: Not performing the same operation on both sides of an equation
Misreading the relationship between symbols or shapes
Forgetting to check the solution by substituting back
Making arithmetic errors when working backwards through multiple steps

topics.title: Word Problems with Variables
identification_cues: A situation described in words with an unknown quantity
You must express relationships mathematically

examples.question: Tom is 3 times as old as Sam. In 6 years, Tom will be twice as old as Sam. How old is Sam now?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Draw a diagram or table for age, distance, or sharing problems
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always define what x represents at the start
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: '5 less than x' is x−5, NOT 5−x
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

steps: Read the question twice — underline what's given and what's asked
Choose the most efficient method (mental, written, or estimation)
Show your working step by step
Check your answer against the original question

worked_example.question: Tom is 3 times as old as Sam. In 6 years, Tom will be twice as old as Sam. How old is Sam now?
steps: Read the question twice — underline what's given and what's asked → Let Sam = x → Tom = 3x
Choose the most efficient method (mental, written, or estimation) → In 6 years: Sam = x+6, Tom = 3x+6
Show your working step by step → Condition: 3x+6 = 2(x+6) → 3x+6 = 2x+12 → x = 6
Check your answer against the original question → Verify: Sam is 6 years old ✓

topics.concept_explanation: A real-world scenario that requires setting up and solving an equation.
common_mistakes: Misinterpreting 'more than' and 'less than' — '5 more than x' is x + 5, not 5 − x
Setting up the wrong equation by misreading the relationships described
Forgetting to answer the actual question — solving for x when the question asks for 2x + 3
Not checking units — make sure the answer matches what was asked (metres, dollars, etc.)

topics.title: Substitution Problems
identification_cues: A symbol, letter-formula, or defined operation is given
You are given specific values to substitute

examples.question: An operation is defined: a★b = 2a² − 3b. Find 4★5.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Write out the substitution explicitly before computing
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Watch the order: in '4★5', confirm which number is a and which is b
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a frequently tested trap in exams. Being aware of it gives you an advantage.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always re-read the definition as you work
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

steps: Read the question twice — underline what's given and what's asked
Choose the most efficient method (mental, written, or estimation)
Show your working step by step
Check your answer against the original question

worked_example.question: An operation is defined: a★b = 2a² − 3b. Find 4★5.
steps: Read the question twice — underline what's given and what's asked → Substitute a=4, b=5: 2(4²) − 3(5)
Choose the most efficient method (mental, written, or estimation) → = 2(16) − 15
Show your working step by step → = 32 − 15 = 17
Check your answer against the original question → Verify: 17 ✓

topics.concept_explanation: A formula or expression is given, and you plug in specific values.
common_mistakes: Substituting values incorrectly — especially with negative numbers in brackets
Not following order of operations after substitution
Confusing which symbol or variable gets which value
Forgetting to square or cube negative values correctly: (−2)² = 4, not −4

topics.title: Speed, Distance & Time (Kinematics)
topics.description: Problems involving objects in motion — finding speed, distance, or time. Aligned with VIC curriculum for selective entry.
topics.concept_explanation: Kinematics is the study of motion without considering forces. In the VIC Selective Entry Exam, you need to solve problems about speed, distance and time — including average speed, relative motion, and multi-leg journeys. These appear as word problems in the Quantitative Reasoning section.

definitions.speed: How fast something moves — distance covered per unit of time (scalar, no direction)
definitions.velocity: Speed with direction (e.g. 60 km/h north) — rarely tested in selective entry but good to know
definitions.displacement: Straight-line distance from start to finish (can be zero if you return to start)
definitions.average_speed: Total distance ÷ Total time for the whole journey
definitions.relative_speed: How fast two objects approach or separate from each other

key_formulas: Distance = Speed × Time   (D = S × T)
Speed = Distance ÷ Time   (S = D / T)
Time = Distance ÷ Speed   (T = D / S)
Average Speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time
km/h → m/s: multiply by 5/18 (or ÷ 3.6)
m/s → km/h: multiply by 18/5 (or × 3.6)
Average speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time. NEVER average the two speeds! This is the most common mistake in selective entry exams.

magic_triangle.description: D = S × T — cover the variable you want to find. Write D on top, S and T on the bottom.
formulas: S = D ÷ T  (divide top by bottom-right)
T = D ÷ S  (divide top by bottom-left)

steps: Read the problem and identify what's given (S, D, or T) and what's asked
Convert all units to match (e.g. minutes → hours, m/s → km/h)
Use D = S × T or its rearrangements
For multi-leg journeys: calculate each leg separately, then combine
Check: does your answer make sense? (speed can't be negative, time should be reasonable)

worked_example.question: A car travels 240 km in 3 hours. What is its speed?
steps: Read the problem and identify what's given (S, D, or T) and what's asked → Speed = Distance ÷ Time
Convert all units to match (e.g. minutes → hours, m/s → km/h) → S = 240 ÷ 3 = 80 km/h
Use D = S × T or its rearrangements
For multi-leg journeys: calculate each leg separately, then combine
Check: does your answer make sense? (speed can't be negative, time should be reasonable) → Verify: 80 km/h ✓

identification_cues: Key words: speed, velocity, rate, distance, travelled, how long, arrive, departed
Involves km/h, m/s, minutes, hours
Mentions vehicles, walking, running, trains, cyclists
Two objects moving toward or away from each other

problem_types.description: Given two of S, D, T — find the third using D = S × T
Multiple segments at different speeds → find total time or average speed
Two objects move toward each other → combined speed = sum of speeds
One object chases another → relative speed = difference of speeds
Same distance at two different speeds → average speed ≠ arithmetic mean
One object leaves earlier → calculate head start distance, then apply relative speed

common_mistakes: Averaging speeds instead of using Total Distance ÷ Total Time
Forgetting to convert units (mixing km/h and m/s, or minutes and hours)
Using speed = distance × time instead of distance ÷ time
Forgetting that 90 minutes = 1.5 hours, not 1.9 hours
In meeting problems: forgetting to add the speeds
Assuming average speed = (speed1 + speed2) ÷ 2 for unequal distances

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always convert units first
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Mixing km/h with minutes or m/s with km causes wrong answers. Convert everything to the same system before calculating.
tips_and_tricks.example: A car goes 90 km/h for 20 minutes → Convert 20 min = 20/60 = 1/3 hour → D = 90 × 1/3 = 30 km

tips_and_tricks.tip: Meeting problems: ADD the speeds
tips_and_tricks.explanation: When two objects move TOWARD each other, the gap closes at the sum of their speeds.
tips_and_tricks.example: Train A at 80 km/h and Train B at 70 km/h approach each other → gap closes at 80 + 70 = 150 km/h

tips_and_tricks.tip: Chasing problems: SUBTRACT the speeds
tips_and_tricks.explanation: When one object chases another in the SAME direction, the gap closes at the difference of their speeds.
tips_and_tricks.example: Car at 100 km/h chases bus at 60 km/h → gap closes at 100 − 60 = 40 km/h

tips_and_tricks.tip: Average speed is NOT the average of speeds
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Average speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time. The arithmetic mean (adding and dividing by 2) only works if the TIME spent at each speed is equal, which is rarely the case.
tips_and_tricks.example: 60 km at 30 km/h then 60 km at 60 km/h → Times: 2h + 1h = 3h → Avg = 120/3 = 40 km/h (NOT 45)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Round trip shortcut: harmonic mean
tips_and_tricks.explanation: For a round trip (same distance, two different speeds), average speed = 2 × s₁ × s₂ ÷ (s₁ + s₂). This is always less than the arithmetic mean.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 60 km/h = 1 km per minute
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This mental shortcut is very useful for quick estimation. Similarly, 120 km/h = 2 km/min, 30 km/h = 0.5 km/min.
tips_and_tricks.example: At 60 km/h, how far in 25 minutes? → 25 × 1 = 25 km (instant answer)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Convert minutes to hours by dividing by 60
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Common time conversions: 15 min = 0.25 h, 20 min = 1/3 h, 30 min = 0.5 h, 45 min = 0.75 h, 90 min = 1.5 h.
tips_and_tricks.example: A journey takes 2 hours 15 minutes = 2.25 hours (not 2.15!)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Draw a timeline or diagram
tips_and_tricks.explanation: For complex problems with multiple objects, departures at different times, or objects passing each other, sketch a simple diagram showing positions and times.
tips_and_tricks.example: Bus leaves at 9:00, car at 9:30 → draw a line showing the bus ahead, mark the gap, show the car catching up

tips_and_tricks.tip: Check your answer with common sense
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Average speed must be between the slowest and fastest speeds. Time cant be negative. If a car goes 60 km/h for 2 hours, it better be 120 km — not 30.
tips_and_tricks.example: If you get average speed = 95 km/h from speeds of 60 and 80, something is wrong (must be between 60 and 80)

tips_and_tricks.tip: For km/h to m/s: divide by 3.6 (or × 5/18)

golden_rules: D = S × T — always start here
Average speed = Total Distance ÷ Total Time (never average the speeds)
Convert ALL units to the same system before calculating

examples.question: A car travels 240 km in 3 hours. What is its speed?
A bus travels at 50 km/h for 4 hours. How far does it go?
How long does it take to travel 150 km at 60 km/h?
Convert 108 km/h to m/s.
A runner covers 200 m in 25 seconds. What is her speed in km/h?
A train travels 180 km at 90 km/h, then 120 km at 60 km/h. What is its average speed?
A cyclist rides to school (6 km) at 15 km/h and returns at 10 km/h. What is the average speed for the round trip?
Two trains are 450 km apart and travel toward each other at 80 km/h and 70 km/h. When do they meet?
solution_steps: Moving toward each other → add speeds

examples.question: A car travels at 80 km/h for 45 minutes, then at 100 km/h for 30 minutes. What is the total distance?
solution_steps: Convert to hours: 45 min = 0.75 h, 30 min = 0.5 h

examples.question: A person walks at 5 km/h for 2 hours, then cycles at 20 km/h for 1.5 hours. What is the average speed?
A train 200 m long passes a pole in 10 seconds. What is its speed in km/h?
Two cities are 360 km apart. A car leaves City A at 8:00 AM at 60 km/h. At what time does it arrive at City B?
A bus leaves Town A at 9:00 AM at 60 km/h. A car leaves the same town at 9:30 AM at 80 km/h. At what time does the car catch the bus?
solution_steps: Bus distance in head start = 60 × 0.5 = 30 km
Time to close 30 km gap = 30 ÷ 20 = 1.5 hours
Car left at 9:30 AM + 1.5 hours = 11:00 AM

examples.question: A boat travels 24 km upstream at 8 km/h and returns downstream at 12 km/h. What is the average speed?
solution_steps: Total distance = 48 km, Total time = 5 hours

examples.question: A train 300 m long passes a platform 500 m long in 40 seconds. What is the speed of the train in km/h?
Two trains, each 150 m long, approach each other at 60 km/h and 90 km/h. How long does it take them to completely pass each other?
A man walks from home to office at 4 km/h and is 10 minutes late. If he walks at 6 km/h, he is 10 minutes early. What is the distance?
A car covers the first half of a journey at 40 km/h and the second half at 60 km/h. What is the average speed?
A and B start at the same point. A walks north at 3 km/h, B walks east at 4 km/h. How far apart are they after 2 hours?
solution_steps: They form a right angle — use Pythagoras

examples.question: A train passes a man walking at 6 km/h in the same direction in 10 seconds. The train is 125 m long. What is the train speed?
solution_steps: Relative speed = train speed − man speed (same direction)
Train speed = relative speed + man speed

examples.question: Two runners start a race. Runner A runs at 8 m/s, Runner B at 6 m/s. A gives B a 40 m head start. After how many seconds does A catch B?
A boat goes 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. It also goes 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream in 13 hours. Find the speed of the boat in still water and the speed of the stream.
solution_steps: Let boat speed = b, stream speed = s
Upstream speed = b − s, Downstream speed = b + s

examples.question: Three cars A, B, C start from the same point. A goes north at 40 km/h, B goes east at 30 km/h, and C goes south at 20 km/h. After 1 hour, how far is A from C? And what is the distance of B from the midpoint of A and C?
solution_steps: After 1 hour: A is 40 km north, B is 30 km east, C is 20 km south
A to C: they go in opposite directions (north and south)
Midpoint of A and C is at (40−20)/2 = 10 km north of start
B is 30 km east of start
examples.answer: A to C: 60 km, B to midpoint: 31.6 km

topics.title: Work & Rate Problems
identification_cues: A task is completed at a certain rate
Multiple workers/pipes/machines combine efforts

examples.question: A tap fills a bath in 10 minutes. A drain empties it in 15 minutes. If both are open, how long to fill an empty bath?
Worker A finishes a job in 6 hours, Worker B in 4 hours. How long if they work together?
A tap fills a tank in 8 hours. A drain empties it in 12 hours. Both open — how long to fill?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Think in terms of 'fraction of job done per unit time'
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: A drain/leak subtracts from the filling rate
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Use LCM to simplify fractions with big numbers

worked_example.question: A tap fills a bath in 10 minutes. A drain empties it in 15 minutes. If both are open, how long to fill an empty bath?
steps: Use LCM to simplify fractions with big numbers → Tap rate: +1/10 per minute (filling)
Answer: 30 minutes

topics.concept_explanation: Multiple workers, pipes, or machines combining efforts to complete a task.
key_formulas: Individual rate = 1 ÷ time to complete the whole job
Combined rate = sum of individual rates
Workers × Days = constant (total work units)
common_mistakes: Adding times directly instead of adding rates — if A takes 3 hours and B takes 6 hours, together ≠ 9 hours
Not converting individual completion times to rates (work per hour) first
Forgetting that more workers means less time (inverse relationship)
Not handling the case where workers have different efficiencies

topics.title: Mixture & Concentration
identification_cues: Two or more substances are mixed
A concentration or percentage of an ingredient is given

examples.question: 20 litres of 30% alcohol is mixed with 10 litres of 60% alcohol. What is the new concentration?
solution_steps: Total alcohol: 12 litres, Total volume: 30 litres

tips_and_tricks.tip: The mixture concentration is always between the two original concentrations (sanity check)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: The larger volume 'pulls' the concentration towards itself
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Write the ratio in the order stated in the question
Simplify by dividing by the HCF
For sharing: find total parts → value of one part → value of each share
For proportion: set up equivalent ratios and cross-multiply

worked_example.question: 20 litres of 30% alcohol is mixed with 10 litres of 60% alcohol. What is the new concentration?
steps: Write the ratio in the order stated in the question → Alcohol in first: 0.30 × 20 = 6 litres
Simplify by dividing by the HCF → Alcohol in second: 0.60 × 10 = 6 litres
For sharing: find total parts → value of one part → value of each share → Total alcohol: 12 litres, Total volume: 30 litres
For proportion: set up equivalent ratios and cross-multiply → Concentration: 12 ÷ 30 = 0.40 = 40%

topics.concept_explanation: Combining two or more substances with different concentrations.
key_formulas: C₁V₁ + C₂V₂ = C_new(V₁ + V₂)
key_rules: Amount of substance = Concentration × Total Volume
common_mistakes: Averaging concentrations instead of using the weighted method
Not tracking the amount of pure substance (concentration × volume) in each part
Confusing the volume of solution with the amount of solute
Forgetting that adding pure water (0% concentration) dilutes the mixture

topics.title: Perimeter & Area
identification_cues: A shape is described or shown
Asked for perimeter (boundary) or area (coverage)

examples.question: A path of width 2 m surrounds a rectangular garden 10 m × 6 m. What is the area of the path?
A rectangular garden is 12 m by 8 m with a 1 m wide path around it. What is the area of the path?
A semicircle has diameter 10 cm. What is its area?

tips_and_tricks.tip: For composite shapes, sketch and label all dimensions
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: A border adds to both sides: 2 m border adds 4 m to length AND 4 m to width
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Perimeter uses the outer boundary only — don't include internal lines
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: don't include internal lines

steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values
Identify the shape type and recall its formula
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable

worked_example.question: A path of width 2 m surrounds a rectangular garden 10 m × 6 m. What is the area of the path?
steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values → Outer rectangle: (10+4) × (6+4) = 14 × 10 = 140 m²
Identify the shape type and recall its formula → Inner garden: 10 × 6 = 60 m²
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes → Area of path: 140 − 60 = 80 m²
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable → Verify: 80 m² ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Finding boundary length or surface coverage of 2D shapes.
key_formulas: Break into simpler parts. Add areas (or subtract for holes).
common_mistakes: Using the wrong height — always use the perpendicular height, not the slant side
Forgetting to halve for triangles or to use πr² (not πd²) for circles
Not breaking composite shapes into simpler components
Confusing perimeter (1D length) with area (2D space) and using wrong units

topics.title: Volume & Surface Area

examples.question: A cylindrical can has radius 5 cm and height 12 cm. Find its volume. (π ≈ 3.14)

tips_and_tricks.tip: Volume is in cubic units (cm³), area is in square units (cm²) — always include the right unit
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: always include the right unit

tips_and_tricks.tip: For surface area: count the number of faces first, then calculate each
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.

steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values
Identify the shape type and recall its formula
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable

worked_example.question: A cylindrical can has radius 5 cm and height 12 cm. Find its volume. (π ≈ 3.14)
steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values → V = πr²h = 3.14 × 25 × 12 = 3.14 × 300 = 942 cm³
Identify the shape type and recall its formula → Apply to this problem
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable → Verify: 942 cm³ ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Finding space inside or total face area of 3D shapes.
identification_cues: Question involves 3D shapes like cubes, rectangular prisms, cylinders, or cones
Asks how much a container can hold (volume) or how much material is needed to cover it (surface area)
Mentions litres, cm³, m³, or asks about capacity
common_mistakes: Confusing volume formulas with surface area formulas
Forgetting the ⅓ factor for pyramids and cones
Not converting between cm³ and litres correctly (1000 cm³ = 1 litre)
Using diameter instead of radius in cylinder or sphere calculations

topics.title: Angles & Lines

angle_facts.rule: = Sum of two opposite interior angles

examples.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One co-interior angle is 115°. Find the other.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Look for Z, F, and C shapes between parallel lines and transversals
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Exterior angle of a triangle is a powerful shortcut
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Index laws let you simplify expressions with powers quickly.
tips_and_tricks.example: x³ × x⁴ = x⁷ (add powers), x⁸ ÷ x³ = x⁵ (subtract powers), (x²)³ = x⁶ (multiply powers)

steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values
Identify the shape type and recall its formula
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable

worked_example.question: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One co-interior angle is 115°. Find the other.
steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values → Co-interior angles sum to 180°
Identify the shape type and recall its formula → Other angle = 180° − 115° = 65°
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable → Verify: 65° ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Finding unknown angles using angle relationships.
identification_cues: Diagram shows intersecting or parallel lines with angles to find
Question involves angle calculations requiring multiple steps
Uses properties of triangles, quadrilaterals, or parallel lines
common_mistakes: Confusing alternate angles (Z-shape, equal) with co-interior angles (C-shape, sum to 180°)
Not recognising all the angle relationships in a complex diagram
Assuming lines are parallel without this being stated
Forgetting that the exterior angle of a triangle = sum of the two opposite interior angles

topics.title: Coordinate Geometry

tips_and_tricks.tip: Count grid squares to check distance calculations where possible
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Gradient = Rise ÷ Run. Up-right = positive gradient
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Gradient = rise ÷ run = (y₂ - y₁) ÷ (x₂ - x₁). Positive = uphill, negative = downhill.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Selective exam level rarely goes beyond midpoint and gradient
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Gradient = rise ÷ run = (y₂ - y₁) ÷ (x₂ - x₁). Positive = uphill, negative = downhill.

examples.question: Find the midpoint of (2, 3) and (8, 7).
What is the gradient of the line through (1, 2) and (4, 11)?

steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values
Identify the shape type and recall its formula
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable

worked_example.question: Find the midpoint of (2, 3) and (8, 7).
steps: Draw or annotate the diagram with all known values → M = ((2+8)/2, (3+7)/2)
Identify the shape type and recall its formula → M = (5, 5)
For compound shapes: break into simple shapes
Check units and whether the answer is reasonable → Verify: (5, 5) ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Working with points on a number plane — midpoint, distance, gradient.
identification_cues: Question shows a coordinate grid or gives coordinates of points
Asks for distances between points, midpoints, or gradients
Involves finding points that satisfy given conditions on a grid
common_mistakes: Mixing up x and y coordinates (x is horizontal, y is vertical)
Making sign errors with negative coordinates
Forgetting to take the square root in the distance formula
Not recognising that gradient = rise/run

topics.title: Tables & Charts
identification_cues: Visual data display is provided
Asked to: read a value, find a total, compare categories, identify trends

tips_and_tricks.tip: Mis-reading the scale is the #1 error — check scale every time
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.
tips_and_tricks.example: check scale every time

tips_and_tricks.tip: For pie charts: central angle for X% = (X/100) × 360°
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Approximately' means you can estimate — don't over-calculate
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Mean = total sum ÷ count. To find a missing value, use: missing = mean × count - known sum.
tips_and_tricks.example: Scores: 8, 6, 9, 7, ? with mean 8 → total needed = 40, sum so far = 30, missing = 10

examples.question: A bar chart shows Mon=12, Tue=8, Wed=15, Thu=10, Fri=5 visitors. What is the mean?
A pie chart shows 25% sport, 40% music, rest is reading. If 200 students surveyed, how many chose reading?

steps: Read the title, axis labels, and legend first
Identify exactly what the question asks (value, trend, comparison)
For calculations: extract the exact numbers from the chart
Watch for scale tricks — axes that don't start at zero

worked_example.question: A bar chart shows Mon=12, Tue=8, Wed=15, Thu=10, Fri=5 visitors. What is the mean?
steps: Read the title, axis labels, and legend first → Total = 12+8+15+10+5 = 50
Identify exactly what the question asks (value, trend, comparison) → Mean = 50/5 = 10
For calculations: extract the exact numbers from the chart
Watch for scale tricks — axes that don't start at zero
Answer: 10 visitors

topics.concept_explanation: Reading and interpreting bar charts, line graphs, pie charts, or data tables.
common_mistakes: Misreading the scale on a graph axis
Not reading column/row headers carefully in tables
Calculating percentage change from the wrong base value
Not checking all the data before answering — the answer may require combining multiple rows/columns

topics.title: Mean, Median, Mode & Range

definitions.how_to_find: Sum of values ÷ count of values
Sort the list; take the middle (or average of two middle values)
The value that appears most often

examples.question: Data: 4, 7, 3, 7, 9, 2, 7, 5. Find mean, median, mode, range.
Test scores: 72, 85, 68, 91, 85, 78. What is the median?
The mean of 5 numbers is 12. Four of them are 8, 10, 14, 16. What is the fifth?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always sort the data before finding median or mode
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Working backwards from mean: missing value = (mean × count) − sum of known
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Mean = total sum ÷ count. To find a missing value, use: missing = mean × count - known sum.
tips_and_tricks.example: Scores: 8, 6, 9, 7, ? with mean 8 → total needed = 40, sum so far = 30, missing = 10

steps: For an even number of values, the median is the average of the two middle values

worked_example.question: Test scores: 72, 85, 68, 91, 85, 78. What is the median?
steps: For an even number of values, the median is the average of the two middle values → Ordered: 68, 72, 78, 85, 85, 91
Answer: 81.5

topics.concept_explanation: Calculating and interpreting measures of central tendency and spread.
key_formulas: Missing value = (mean × count) − sum of known values
identification_cues: Question asks for the mean, median, mode, or range of a data set
Involves finding a missing value given the mean or median
Data may be presented in a frequency table rather than a simple list
common_mistakes: Not ordering data before finding the median
Using the wrong formula for mean from a frequency table (sum of f×x, divided by sum of f)
Confusing range (max − min) with interquartile range
Not considering whether adding or removing a value changes the median position

topics.title: Probability
identification_cues: Involves random events, chance, likelihood
Key words: probability, chance, likelihood, at random, fair
probability_rules: P(A and B) for independent events = P(A) × P(B)
P(A or B) for mutually exclusive events = P(A) + P(B)

examples.question: A bag has 4 red and 6 blue marbles. Two drawn without replacement. P(both red)?
A bag has 3 red, 4 blue, 5 green marbles. What is P(not green)?
A coin is flipped 3 times. What is P(exactly 2 heads)?
Two dice are rolled. P(sum > 9)?

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Without replacement' means the denominator decreases on the second draw
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Mean = total sum ÷ count. To find a missing value, use: missing = mean × count - known sum.
tips_and_tricks.example: Scores: 8, 6, 9, 7, ? with mean 8 → total needed = 40, sum so far = 30, missing = 10

tips_and_tricks.tip: All probabilities in a sample space must add to 1
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Use a probability tree for two-stage problems

worked_example.question: A bag has 3 red, 4 blue, 5 green marbles. What is P(not green)?
steps: Use a probability tree for two-stage problems → Total = 12
Answer: 7/12

topics.concept_explanation: Finding the likelihood of random events, including two-stage probability.
key_formulas: P(event) = Number of favourable outcomes ÷ Total number of outcomes
Expected outcomes = Probability × Number of trials
common_mistakes: Not listing all possible outcomes systematically
Confusing 'with replacement' and 'without replacement'
Adding probabilities when you should multiply (AND events)
Forgetting that probabilities must be between 0 and 1

topics.title: Venn Diagrams & Sets
identification_cues: Overlapping categories or groups
Key words: both, neither, only one, at least one, total

examples.question: 30 students: 18 like maths, 15 like science, 7 like both. How many like neither?
In a class of 40: 25 play cricket, 18 play soccer, 8 play both. How many play neither?
30 students: 18 like maths, 15 like science, 20 like English. 10 like maths & science, 8 like maths & English, 7 like science & English, 5 like all three. How many like exactly one subject?

tips_and_tricks.tip: Always draw the Venn diagram — even a quick sketch saves errors
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.
tips_and_tricks.example: even a quick sketch saves errors

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'At least one' = Total − neither
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

steps: Start filling from the innermost region (both/all) and work outward

worked_example.question: In a class of 40: 25 play cricket, 18 play soccer, 8 play both. How many play neither?
steps: Start filling from the innermost region (both/all) and work outward → Cricket only = 25−8 = 17

topics.concept_explanation: Problems involving overlapping groups or categories.
key_formulas: n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) − n(A ∩ B)
common_mistakes: Double-counting the intersection — it's already included in each circle's total
Not starting from the intersection when filling in a Venn diagram
Forgetting those in 'neither' set (outside all circles)
Confusing 'A only' (in A but not B) with 'A' (in A, possibly also in B)

topics.title: Matrix / Grid Problems

tips_and_tricks.tip: Mark what CANNOT go in a cell — the 'can't be' approach is often faster
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a common mistake that costs marks in exams. Make sure you understand the difference.

tips_and_tricks.tip: For 4×4 grids: each row and column must contain each value once
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

examples.question: In a 3×3 grid, each row, column, and diagonal sums to 15. Fill in: [2,?,6], [?,5,?], [?,?,?]
solution_steps: Continue using sum=15 for each line

steps: Organise information in a table, grid, or Venn diagram
Start with the most restrictive clue
Use elimination: cross out impossibilities
Check that every clue is satisfied by your answer

worked_example.question: In a 3×3 grid, each row, column, and diagonal sums to 15. Fill in: [2,?,6], [?,5,?], [?,?,?]
steps: Organise information in a table, grid, or Venn diagram → Row 1: 2+?+6=15 → ?=7
Start with the most restrictive clue → Col 1: 2+?+?=15
Use elimination: cross out impossibilities → Diagonal: 2+5+?=15 → ?=8 (bottom right)
Check that every clue is satisfied by your answer → Continue using sum=15 for each line

topics.concept_explanation: Grids where rows and columns have constraints (e.g., each value appears exactly once).
identification_cues: Numbers are arranged in a grid or table with a missing value
Rows, columns, or diagonals follow a consistent mathematical rule
May involve magic squares, cross-number puzzles, or operation grids
common_mistakes: Assuming the rule applies only to rows — always check columns and diagonals too
Not testing the discovered rule against ALL available entries
Overlooking that the operation might vary (e.g., row 1 uses addition, row 2 uses multiplication)
Making arithmetic errors when verifying the pattern

topics.title: Logical Deduction
identification_cues: A narrative with constraints or clues
You need to find a unique arrangement or conclusion

tips_and_tricks.tip: Read all clues before starting — a later clue may instantly resolve an earlier one
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.
tips_and_tricks.example: a later clue may instantly resolve an earlier one

tips_and_tricks.tip: Work with the most specific clues first
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'Not next to' and 'immediately to the left of' are powerful positional constraints
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Index laws let you simplify expressions with powers quickly.
tips_and_tricks.example: x³ × x⁴ = x⁷ (add powers), x⁸ ÷ x³ = x⁵ (subtract powers), (x²)³ = x⁶ (multiply powers)

examples.question: All cats are animals. Some animals are pets. Which must be true? A) All cats are pets B) Some cats may be pets C) No cats are pets
solution_steps: 'Some animals are pets' — but not necessarily cats
Some cats COULD be pets, but it's not guaranteed
B is the only safe conclusion
examples.answer: B) Some cats may be pets

steps: Organise information in a table, grid, or Venn diagram
Start with the most restrictive clue
Use elimination: cross out impossibilities
Check that every clue is satisfied by your answer

worked_example.question: All cats are animals. Some animals are pets. Which must be true? A) All cats are pets B) Some cats may be pets C) No cats are pets
steps: Organise information in a table, grid, or Venn diagram → 'All cats are animals' ✓
Start with the most restrictive clue → 'Some animals are pets' — but not necessarily cats
Use elimination: cross out impossibilities → Some cats COULD be pets, but it's not guaranteed
Check that every clue is satisfied by your answer → B is the only safe conclusion
Answer: B) Some cats may be pets
worked_example.answer: B) Some cats may be pets

topics.concept_explanation: A set of clues about people, objects, or events — deduce who did what or what goes where.
common_mistakes: Not using a systematic approach (grid or table) to track all possibilities
Missing a clue or not fully utilising all given information
Making an early assumption that contradicts a later clue
Not double-checking the final answer against ALL clues

topics.title: Time Problems

tips_and_tricks.tip: Convert time to a consistent unit before calculating
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Always convert to matching units before calculating. Common: 1 km = 1000 m, 1 hr = 60 min.
tips_and_tricks.example: 3.5 km in metres = 3.5 × 1000 = 3500 m. 90 minutes in hours = 90 ÷ 60 = 1.5 hours

tips_and_tricks.tip: Watch for AM/PM crossovers in timetable problems
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a frequently tested trap in exams. Being aware of it gives you an advantage.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'How many complete weeks in N days?' → N ÷ 7 (integer division)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

examples.question: A train departs at 8:45 am and arrives at 11:20 am. How long is the journey?
It is 3:50 pm in Melbourne (AEST, UTC+10). What time is it in London (UTC)?
A bus leaves every 12 minutes. The first bus is at 6:15 am. When is the 5th bus?

steps: Convert all time to the same unit (usually hours or minutes)
For money problems: track carefully using a table
For interest: Simple = P×r×t, Compound = P(1+r)ᵗ
Check: does the final amount make sense?

worked_example.question: A train departs at 8:45 am and arrives at 11:20 am. How long is the journey?
steps: Convert all time to the same unit (usually hours or minutes) → 8:45 → 9:00 = 15 min
For money problems: track carefully using a table → 9:00 → 11:00 = 2 hours
For interest: Simple = P×r×t, Compound = P(1+r)ᵗ → 11:00 → 11:20 = 20 min
Check: does the final amount make sense? → Total = 2 h 35 min
Answer: 2 hours 35 minutes

key_formulas: Convert everything to minutes, subtract, then convert back
key_rules: 1 minute = 60 seconds
1 hour = 60 minutes
1 day = 24 hours
1 week = 7 days
1 year = 365 days (366 in leap year)
topics.concept_explanation: Time problems involve calculating durations, converting between time formats (12-hour, 24-hour), and solving problems involving schedules, time zones, and elapsed time. They require careful handling of the base-60 system.
identification_cues: Question involves clocks, schedules, timetables, or time calculations
Asks for elapsed time, arrival time, or time differences
Involves converting between hours, minutes, and seconds
common_mistakes: Not accounting for the base-60 system: 1 hour = 60 minutes, not 100
Errors when crossing midnight (12:00 AM) or noon (12:00 PM) in calculations
Confusing AM and PM or 12-hour and 24-hour formats
Not considering time zone differences in problems involving different locations

topics.title: Money & Finance

tips_and_tricks.tip: Percentage profit/loss is always calculated on the COST PRICE, not the selling price
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a reliable rule to follow — it applies consistently in exam problems.

tips_and_tricks.tip: 'How much change?' → subtract price from amount given
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Applying this consistently improves both speed and accuracy in exam problems.

examples.question: A shirt costs $45 after a 25% discount. What was the original price?
$500 invested at 4% simple interest for 3 years. Total amount?
Buy 3 items at $12.50 each with a 'buy 2 get 1 half price' deal. Total cost?

steps: Convert all time to the same unit (usually hours or minutes)
For money problems: track carefully using a table
For interest: Simple = P×r×t, Compound = P(1+r)ᵗ
Check: does the final amount make sense?

worked_example.question: $500 invested at 4% simple interest for 3 years. Total amount?
steps: Convert all time to the same unit (usually hours or minutes) → Interest = 500 × 0.04 × 3 = $60
For money problems: track carefully using a table → Total = 500 + 60 = $560
For interest: Simple = P×r×t, Compound = P(1+r)ᵗ
Check: does the final amount make sense? → Verify: $560 ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Money problems in quantitative reasoning involve calculating costs, change, discounts, profit/loss, and best-value comparisons. They test practical numeracy in real-world financial contexts.
identification_cues: Question involves prices, costs, change, or financial calculations
Asks for total cost, best value, or how much money is needed
Involves discounts, taxes, tips, or currency conversions
common_mistakes: Rounding intermediate calculations and accumulating rounding errors
Forgetting to include all costs (e.g., tax, delivery, or multiple items)
Confusing 'per unit cost' comparisons when package sizes differ
Not reading whether prices include or exclude GST/tax

topics.title: Mental Math Speed Tricks
key_rules: ×11: write digit, add adjacent pairs, write last digit. E.g., 72×11 = 7_(7+2)_2 = 792
Square numbers ending in 5: n5² = n×(n+1) followed by 25. E.g., 35² = 3×4=12, append 25 → 1225

examples.question: Calculate 64 × 25 mentally.
What is 15% of 240?
Calculate 73 × 11 mentally.
Which is larger: 3/7 or 5/12?
Estimate: 389 × 0.48
Without calculating exactly: 49² − 48² = ?
solution_steps: Use difference of squares: a² − b² = (a+b)(a−b)

tips_and_tricks.tip: For comparing fractions: cross-multiply and compare (avoids finding common denominators)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: You can only add or subtract fractions when they have the same denominator.

tips_and_tricks.tip: To check divisibility by 3: sum the digits — if sum is divisible by 3, so is the number
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.
tips_and_tricks.example: if sum is divisible by 3, so is the number

tips_and_tricks.tip: For 'which is closest' questions: estimate first, then check only the close options
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Round numbers to 1 significant figure for quick estimation, then compare with your exact answer.
tips_and_tricks.example: 49 × 21 ≈ 50 × 20 = 1000. Exact: 1029. Close enough — your calculation is likely correct.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Percentage increase/decrease: multiply by (1 + rate) or (1 − rate)
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Percentage change = (difference ÷ original) × 100. Always divide by the ORIGINAL value.
tips_and_tricks.example: Price rises from $40 to $50: change = 10, % change = (10/40) × 100 = 25% increase

steps: Identify the rate (per hour, per minute, per item)
Use the formula: Work = Rate × Time
For combined work: add the rates, not the times
Convert all units to match before calculating

worked_example.question: Calculate 64 × 25 mentally.
steps: Identify the rate (per hour, per minute, per item) → 64 × 25 = 64 ÷ 4 × 100
Use the formula: Work = Rate × Time → 64 ÷ 4 = 16
For combined work: add the rates, not the times → 16 × 100 = 1600
Convert all units to match before calculating

topics.concept_explanation: Shortcuts for fast calculation without a calculator — essential for the 40-second-per-question time limit.
identification_cues: Calculations that seem complex but have shortcuts
Multiplying or dividing by numbers close to round values (99, 25, 50)
Questions designed to test speed more than complexity
common_mistakes: Applying a trick incorrectly — always verify with estimation
Trying to use mental tricks when the standard method would be faster
Making errors in the compensation step (e.g., 99 × 7 = 100×7 − 7 = 693, not 703)
Not practising enough — tricks only save time when they are automatic

topics.title: MCQ Elimination & Exam Strategy

examples.question: If 3x + 5 = 20, what is the value of 6x + 10? Options: A) 15, B) 30, C) 40, D) 45
solution_steps: Trap: solving for x first (x=5) then computing is slower
Shortcut: recognise the expression is double the original

examples.question: A price increases by 25% then decreases by 20%. The final price compared to original is: A) Same, B) Higher, C) Lower, D) Cannot tell
solution_steps: Same! Trap: students assume increase then decrease cancels unevenly

examples.question: Which is NOT a factor of 144? A) 8, B) 9, C) 14, D) 16

tips_and_tricks.tip: Time allocation: 30 seconds to solve + 10 seconds to verify = 40 seconds per question
tips_and_tricks.explanation: A quick verification step catches careless errors and can save marks.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Back-substitution: plug each option into the question — one will work
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Plug your answer back into the original equation to verify it works.

tips_and_tricks.tip: Watch for 'almost right' answers — they're usually based on a common error
tips_and_tricks.explanation: This is a frequently tested trap in exams. Being aware of it gives you an advantage.
tips_and_tricks.example: they're usually based on a common error

tips_and_tricks.tip: Do easy questions first, hard ones last — all questions are worth the same marks
tips_and_tricks.explanation: Getting this step right early prevents cascading errors through the rest of the solution.
tips_and_tricks.example: all questions are worth the same marks

steps: If you can eliminate 2 options, guessing from the remaining 2-3 gives 33-50% chance

worked_example.question: Which is NOT a factor of 144? A) 8, B) 9, C) 14, D) 16
steps: If you can eliminate 2 options, guessing from the remaining 2-3 gives 33-50% chance → 144/8 = 18 ✓

topics.concept_explanation: Techniques for maximising score on multiple-choice quantitative reasoning exams under time pressure.
common_mistakes: Trap: Giving the answer to a DIFFERENT question than asked (e.g., finding x when asked for 2x)
Trap: Sign errors — an option with the right number but wrong sign is a distractor
Trap: Forgetting to convert units (cm vs m, seconds vs minutes)
Trap: 'None of the above' — only choose this after verifying all others are wrong
Trap: Percentage increase ≠ percentage decrease (20% up then 20% down ≠ original)
identification_cues: Any multiple-choice question where you can eliminate options without full calculation
Questions with widely spread answer options suited to estimation
Complex problems where working backwards from answer choices is faster

before_exam_checklist: Know all key formulas by heart
Practise doing calculations mentally — speed matters
Do timed practice papers under real conditions
Review incorrect answers to understand WHY, not just what the correct answer was

time_management: Scan all questions first — attempt easy questions first
Spend no more than ~45 seconds on any single question before moving on
If stuck, eliminate wrong answers and make a best guess, then return if time allows
reading_questions: Underline key words: NOT, LEAST, MORE THAN, TOTAL
Check what unit the answer should be in
Re-read the question after getting an answer — did you answer what was actually asked?
checking_answers: Does the answer make sense in context?
Is the answer in the right ballpark? Estimate first.
Did you use the right formula?

common_traps.example: % increase using new price not original

common_traps.trap: Forgetting border adds to both sides
common_traps.example: Adding path width once instead of twice
common_traps.fix: A border of width w adds 2w to length AND 2w to width

common_traps.trap: Averaging speeds instead of using D÷T

common_traps.example: Finding middle value of unsorted list
Finding what IS true when asked what isn't
common_traps.fix: Circle the word NOT in the question
